1: Mi khe-chakham u-mi yode'a pesher davar — 'Who is like the wise man? Who else knows the meaning of a matter?' Pesher (H6592, interpretation/meaning) is an Aramaic loanword that appears in later Hebrew (also Dan 2:4-7:28 throughout; and in the Dead Sea Scrolls' pesher-commentaries). Its presence here is one of the linguistic markers suggesting a Persian-period date for Ecclesiastes' final form. Chokhemat adam ta'ir panav — 'a person's wisdom brightens his face.' Ya'ir (brightens/illuminates) — the face lit by wisdom is the visible sign of inner understanding. Ve-az panav yeshuneh — 'and transforms its harsh expression' — literally 'the strength/boldness of his face is changed.' Wisdom changes not just what one knows but how one appears: the harsh or defensive expression softens under wisdom's light.
2: Ani pi-melek shmor — 'I counsel you: keep the king's command.' The abrupt opening in Hebrew — 'I' + 'the king's mouth/command' + 'keep' — is elliptical. The MKT supplies 'counsel you' for clarity. Ve-al divrat shevuat Elohim — 'and do so because of your oath before God.' The counsel to royal obedience is grounded not in the king's power alone but in the oath sworn before God — a theological foundation for political compliance. This connects to the covenant context of royal service in Israel.
3: Al-tibahal mi-panav telekh — 'Do not be in a hurry to leave the king's presence.' Bahal (hasty/dismayed) — the third use of this word in Ecclesiastes (5:2; 7:9): hasty withdrawal from the king, like hasty speech before God (5:2) or hasty anger (7:9), is a form of rashness. Al-ta'amod be-davar ra — 'do not persist in a bad cause.' The counsel is practical political wisdom: do not rush out of the king's sight, and do not lock into a bad position. Ki kol-asher yachpots ya'aseh — 'for he does whatever he wants.' The king's absolute will is the given context within which the courtier must navigate.
4: Ba'asher devar-melek shilton — 'For the king's word is absolute.' Shilton (H7983, power/dominion/authority) — a word used in the Aramaic portions of Daniel for royal sovereignty. U-mi yomar-lo mah-ta'aseh — 'and no one can say to him, “What are you doing?“' The rhetorical question borrows language from Job 9:12 ('who can say to him, “What are you doing?“') where it describes God's absolute sovereignty. The Teacher uses the divine-sovereignty formula for the king — not to equate king with God, but to describe the practical absolutism of royal power within the sub-eschatological frame.
5: Shomer mitzvah lo yeda davar ra — 'Whoever obeys his command will come to no harm.' The practical promise: compliance brings safety, not because the king is always just, but because defiance of absolute power is self-destructive. Ve-et u-mishpat yeda lev chakham — 'and the wise heart will know the proper time and way.' Et u-mishpat (time and judgment/method) — the two-part discernment that wisdom provides: when to act (et) and how to act (mishpat). The et connects to the times poem of ch 3; wisdom knows which time calls for what response.
6: Ki le-khol-chefetz yesh et u-mishpat — 'For every purpose has its proper time and method.' Chefetz (H2656, purpose/delight/matter) — the word from 3:1's introduction to the times poem. The et (time) and mishpat (method/judgment) pair recurs: every matter has both its moment and its appropriate mode. Ki ra'at ha'adam rabbah alav — 'though a person's trouble weighs heavily on him.' The contextual complication: knowing that there is a proper time and way is not the same as knowing when and how in the specific situation. The trouble of the human condition is precisely this gap between principle and application.
7: Ki einennu yode'a mah-sheyih'yeh — 'For no one knows what is coming.' Mi yode'a (who knows?) / einennu yode'a (he does not know) — the epistemological limit formula recurs: the future is opaque. Ki ka'asher yih'yeh mi yagid-lo — 'and who can tell him how it will come?' The double question structure: not just what will happen, but how it will come. The king's power (vv.3-4) does not exempt the king from this epistemological limit; even the one who 'does whatever he wants' cannot know the future.
8: Ein adam shaliyt ba-ruach likhloa et-ha-ruach — 'No one has power over the life-breath to restrain it.' The word ruach (H7307, spirit/breath/wind) is the key word here: in the context of death, ruach is the life-breath that God gives and takes (3:21; Gen 2:7; 6:17; Job 34:14-15). No human shaliyt (ruler/controller) has authority over this ruach. Ve-ein shilton be-yom ha-mavet — 'and no one has power over the day of death.' Shilton reappears (v.4: the king's shilton); neither the absolute monarch nor anyone else has shilton over death's timing. Ve-ein mishlachat ba-milchamah — 'there is no release from that battle.' The war metaphor: death is a battle in which the soldier cannot be discharged or furloughed.
9: Et-kol-zeh ra'iti ve-naton et-libbi le-khol-ma'aseh asher na'asah tahat hashemesh — 'All this I observed while applying my mind to everything done under the sun.' The empirical formula marks the transition to a new social observation. Et asher shalat ha'adam ba'adam le-ra' lo — 'a time when one person dominates another to their own harm.' The verb shalat (to rule/control) — the same word as the king's shilton (vv.4, 8): now the king's type of absolute control is exercised by one person over another, with harmful results. The le-ra' lo (to his own harm) is ambiguous: to the oppressor's harm, or to the oppressed's harm? The ambiguity may be intentional.
10: U-ve-khein ra'iti resha'im qeburim u-va'u — 'Then I saw the wicked buried.' The observation of wicked people receiving honorable burial: u-va'u (and they went/came) from bo — 'they came and went.' U-min-maqom qadosh yehalekhu — 'they had gone from the sacred place' — possibly the temple or a sacred assembly where they had appeared religious. Va-yishtakkehu va-'ir khen asher ken-pa'alu — 'and they were soon forgotten in the very city where they had done such things' — the wicked receive burial (honor) and then are forgotten in the city of their crimes. Gam-zeh havel — the verdict: the apparent dignity of the wicked's burial and the forgetting of their crimes in the same city are both instances of hevel.
11: Asher ein-na'asah fitgam ma'aseh ha-ra'ah mehera — 'When the sentence for a wrong is not carried out quickly.' The word fitgam (H6599, sentence/decree/verdict) is an Aramaic loanword used in Ezra and Esther for royal decrees. Its appearance in Ecclesiastes is another Persian-period linguistic marker. Al-kein male lev benei ha-adam bahem la'asot ra — 'the human heart is emboldened to do evil.' Male lev (the heart is filled/full) — when swift judicial response to wrongdoing is absent, the human heart fills with confidence to continue wrongdoing. The verse is a precise observation about deterrence: justice delayed enables sin emboldened.
12: Asher chote oseh ra me'at ve-ma'arikh lo — 'Although a sinner does evil a hundred times and still lives long.' Me'at (a hundred times) — the sinner's multiplied wrongdoing without apparent consequence. Ki gam-yode'a ani asher yih'yeh tov le-yire'ei ha'Elohim asher yir'u milfanav — 'I know that it will go well with those who fear God, who stand in awe before him.' Despite the evidence of delayed justice and wicked longevity, the Teacher affirms confidence in the God-fearer's outcome. The phrase yir'u milfanav (stand in awe before him) intensifies yire'ei ha'Elohim (those who fear God): it is not formal religion but personal reverence.
13: Ve-tov lo yih'yeh la-rasha — 'But it will not go well with the wicked.' The simple negation answers v.12's positive. Ve-lo ya'arikh yamim ka-tzel — 'and their days will not lengthen like a shadow.' The tzel (shadow) — connected to the wisdom-shadow of 7:12 (be-tzel ha-chakhmah): wisdom provides protective shade; the wicked's days are not extended like a protective shadow. Asher einennu yare' mi-lifnei Elohim — 'because they do not fear God.' The absence of yir'at Elohim (fear of God) is the identifying characteristic of the wicked: not specific sins but the foundational orientation away from God.
14: Yesh-hevel asher na'asah al-ha'aretz — 'There is a futility that occurs on earth.' The Teacher's flat observation of injustice in outcomes: yesh tzadiqim asher magiy'a alehem ke-ma'aseh ha-resha'im — 'the righteous get what the wicked deserve.' The moral categories (tzadiq, righteous; rasha, wicked) that the wisdom tradition uses to predict outcomes are here inverted in observed experience. Ve-yesh resha'im shemagi'a alehem ke-ma'aseh ha-tzadiqim — 'and the wicked get what the righteous deserve.' The double inversion is complete. Amarti shegam-zeh havel — 'I said that this too is futility.' The Teacher does not explain or rationalize the injustice; he names it hevel and continues.
15: Ve-shivachthi ani et-ha-simha — 'So I commend joy.' Shivach (H7623, to praise/commend/declare fortunate) — the same verb as 4:2 ('I counted the dead more fortunate than the living'). Here it is used positively: the Teacher publicly commends joy as the response to the observed injustice of vv.12-14. Asher ein-tov la'adam tahat hashemesh ki im-le'ekhol ve-lishtot ve-lismoch — 'for there is nothing better for people under the sun than to eat and drink and be glad.' The carpe diem formula recurs in its fullest form. Ve-hu yilavennu ba-amalo — 'this accompanies them in their work through all the days of life that God has given them under the sun' — joy is not an escape from work but the companion of work throughout the God-given life.
16: Ka'asher natati et-libbi lada'at chokhmah — 'When I applied my mind to know wisdom.' The Teacher's empirical method statement before the conclusion of this section. Ve-lir'ot et-ha-inyan asher na'asah al-ha'aretz — 'and to observe all the activity carried on upon the earth.' Inyan (business/occupation/activity) — recurs from 1:13 for the 'unhappy business' of human investigation. Ki gam ba-yom u-va-layla shena be-einav einennu ro'eh — 'for neither day nor night do people see sleep.' The extreme of wakeful investigation: not even sleep interrupts the effort to understand. This is the hyperbolic description of comprehensive inquiry.
17: Ve-ra'iti et-kol-ma'aseh ha'Elohim ki lo-yuchal ha'adam limtzo et-ha-ma'aseh asher na'asah tahat hashemesh — 'Then I saw all the work of God — that no one can discover what is done under the sun.' The phrase kol ma'aseh ha'Elohim (all the work of God) is the comprehensive divine activity that the Teacher claims no one can fully discover. Be-sheliyiqchal ha'adam la'amol levaqesh ve-lo yimtza — 'no matter how much one labors in searching, they will not find it out.' Amal le-baqesh (labor in searching) — the word amal applied to the activity of inquiry: the labor of investigation itself is subject to the same futility-verdict as physical labor. Ve-gam im-yomar he-chakham lada'at lo yuchal limtzo — 'even if the wise claim to know, they cannot find it out.' Even the self-proclaimed expert is bounded by the same epistemological limit. This is the Teacher's most comprehensive statement of the limits of 'under the sun' wisdom.
